guide.briefingNote: Start with one specific place, routine, or object rather than a broad life-summary question. Let the interviewee set the pace and stay with the detail that feels most alive to them. If they correct something or take the story in a different direction, treat that as useful information. Silence is fine. You do not need to fill every pause. If photographs or objects come up, use them as gentle prompts rather than proof points.
openingLines: I've been wanting to ask you properly about growing up in Lanarkshire—what it was really like day to day.
There's so much I want to know about your life that I've only heard bits of.
I'm going to ask you quite a lot of questions today, but you can tell me if there's anything you'd rather not talk about.
sensitivityNotes: If something feels uncertain, ask gently and let them correct or reshape it.
If a topic feels tender, pause and follow their lead rather than pressing on.
If memory is vague on dates or order, stay with the detail they do remember.
If they seem tired or want to stop, that is the right moment to pause or finish.
If photographs or objects appear, use them as gentle prompts rather than testing memory against them.

sections.title: Childhood and place
sections.intro: Let's start with where you grew up and what the place felt like day to day.

questions.text: What do you remember about the house or the street where you lived when you were small?
What was it like outside—the fields, the pit, the roads, the shops nearby?
Can you remember what time of year felt different? Summer, winter, anything that stands out?
Who else lived close by—neighbours, other families—and what were they like?

followUps: Do you remember what the inside of the house smelled like, or what the light was like?
Were there places you weren't allowed to go, or places that felt dangerous?
What did you do when the weather was bad and you had to stay indoors?
Did you have a favourite spot—somewhere you'd go to be on your own or with a friend?

sections.title: Family and relationships
sections.intro: Now let's talk about the people around you and what family life was like.

questions.text: Tell me about your mother and father—what were they like as people?
Did you have brothers or sisters, and what was it like living with them?
Who in the family did you feel closest to, or who made you laugh?
What was mealtimes like—did you all eat together, and what do you remember about the food?

followUps: What would your father do or say that you still remember clearly?
Were there any family sayings or jokes that got repeated?
Did anyone in the family have a particular skill or talent that impressed you?
Who looked after you if your parents were out, or who did you turn to if you were upset?

sections.title: Work and daily life
sections.intro: I'd like to hear about how you spent your days, and about any work that became part of your life.

questions.text: What did your father do for work, and do you remember anything about what that meant for the family?
What were your own jobs or chores when you were growing up—what did you have to do?
You worked in a shop in Glasgow later on—how did that come about, and what was that like?
What did a normal day look like for you—how did you spend your time?

followUps: Did you go to school, and what do you remember about that?
Were there things you had to do that you really didn't like?
What was the shop like—the people, the goods, the customers, the owners?
Did you have any time that was just yours, or did you always have something you needed to be doing?

sections.title: Hard times and major events
sections.intro: Every life has stretches that are harder or more uncertain. If any come to mind, we can talk about those gently.

questions.text: Were there times when money was particularly tight, or when you went without something you needed?
Did anything happen—an illness, a loss, an accident—that changed things for your family?
Looking back, was there a time when you felt scared or uncertain about what would happen next?

followUps: How did your parents manage when things were difficult—what did they do?
Did the community help each other out, or was it mostly every family for themselves?
Did you understand at the time what was happening, or did you only make sense of it later?

sections.title: Objects, photos and remembered stories
sections.intro: Sometimes a photograph, keepsake, or family story unlocks detail that nothing else does.

questions.text: Is there a photograph from when you were young that you still have, or that you remember clearly?
Do you have anything from that time—something you've kept or that's been kept for you?
Is there a story about your mother or father or grandparents that got told again and again in your family?
What's a story about yourself as a child that people still bring up?

followUps: What was happening in that photograph—do you remember the day it was taken?
Why do you think that object mattered enough to keep?
Who told that story, and what did they always emphasize about it?
Do you believe that story the same way now, or has it changed for you?

sections.title: Looking back
sections.intro: As you look back now, I'd like to hear what stands out and what still feels important.

questions.text: When you think about that time now, what do you miss about it?
What are you glad you don't have to do anymore?
What do you think was the most important thing that happened to you, or that shaped who you became?

followUps: Is there anything you wish you'd done differently, or anything you wish you'd known then?
What would you want your grandchildren to know about what life was like for you?
Looking at everything you've lived through since then, how do you make sense of those early years now?

guide.briefingNote: Start with one specific place, routine, or object rather than a broad life-summary question. Let the interviewee set the pace and stay with the detail that feels most alive to them. If they correct something or take the story in a different direction, treat that as useful information. Silence is fine. You do not need to fill every pause. If photographs or objects come up, use them as gentle prompts rather than proof points.
openingLines: I'm so glad we're doing this, Brigid. I want to hear about your life in your own words—the place you grew up, the people, the work, all of it.
There's no rush and no wrong answers. If something's hard to talk about, we can go gently, or skip it altogether.
I'm recording this so I don't miss anything, and so the family can hear your voice the way it really sounds.
sensitivityNotes: If something feels uncertain, ask gently and let them correct or reshape it.
If a topic feels tender, pause and follow their lead rather than pressing on.
If memory is vague on dates or order, stay with the detail they do remember.
If they seem tired or want to stop, that is the right moment to pause or finish.
If wartime or shortage memories come up, let them describe what felt ordinary or difficult in their own terms.

sections.title: Childhood and place
sections.intro: Let's start with where you grew up and what the place felt like day to day.

questions.text: Can you tell me the name of the place where you grew up, and what kind of land it was—was it a farm, a village, near water?
What's one thing about that place that comes back to you now—a smell, a sound, the way the light was at a certain time of day?
Where did you sleep as a child, and who else was in the house with you?
When you think of being a child there, was it mostly outdoors or indoors, or mixed?

followUps: Can you describe what the house itself looked like—the rooms, the kitchen, anything you remember about how it felt to be inside?
Were there neighbours close by, or was it more isolated?
What season do you remember most clearly from childhood—winter, summer, spring?
Did you have a favourite place to go or be, somewhere you'd escape to or spend time?

sections.title: Family and relationships
sections.intro: Now let's talk about the people around you and what family life was like.

questions.text: How many brothers and sisters did you have, and where did you fall in the line—were you oldest, youngest, somewhere in the middle?
What was your father like, and what do you remember about what he did day to day?
Tell me about your mother—what was she like, and what was her day usually like?
Did any of your siblings leave Ireland, and if so, where did they go and when?

followUps: What was the mood in the house when emigration was being talked about—was it something people wanted, or something they felt they had to do?
Do you remember what your mother or father said about the ones who left?
Was there a particular sibling you were close to, or who you missed?
Did anyone come back to visit, or did you hear from them after they left?

sections.title: Work, beliefs and daily life
sections.intro: I'd like to hear about how you spent your days, any work that shaped your life, and whether belief or church was part of ordinary life around you.

questions.text: What work did you do as a child and young person—on the farm, in the house, or elsewhere?
What time did you get up, and what was the first thing you'd do?
How often did you go to Mass or church, and what was that like—was it something you looked forward to, or just part of the routine?
Were there rules in your house about what you could or couldn't do, especially around food, days of the week, or behaviour?

followUps: Who taught you how to do the work—was it your mother, your father, an older sibling?
What was the hardest or most tiring part of the day's work?
Do you remember a priest or nun who made an impression on you?
Were there times when the rules felt strict, or times when people bent them?

sections.title: Hard times and major events
sections.intro: Every life has stretches that are harder or more uncertain. If any come to mind, we can talk about those gently.

questions.text: Was there a time when things were particularly hard—illness, loss, hunger, or something else?
Did anything happen that changed your family or your life in a big way?
Looking back, was there a moment when you had to grow up quickly or take on more responsibility?

followUps: How did your family cope with that, and who helped or supported you?
Do you remember what you felt at the time, and how you feel about it now?
Did that change how you saw yourself or what you wanted for your future?

sections.title: Objects, photos and remembered stories
sections.intro: Sometimes a photograph, keepsake, or family story unlocks detail that nothing else does.

questions.text: Is there a photograph from that time that you still have, or that you remember being taken?
Do you have anything from childhood or early life that you've kept—something from the house, or something someone gave you?
Is there a story about one of your parents or grandparents that got told and retold in the family?
Was there a saying or a piece of advice that someone in your family used to repeat?

followUps: What do you remember about that photograph—who was in it, where it was taken, what were you wearing or doing?
Why have you kept that object, and what does it mean to you now?
Do you remember who told you that story, and why they told it?
Do you find yourself saying that thing now, or did it stick with you in some other way?

sections.title: Looking back
sections.intro: As you look back now, I'd like to hear what stands out and what still feels important.

questions.text: When you think about that time in your life now, what do you feel—is it mostly good memories, or is it mixed?
Is there something from back then that you wish had been different?
What do you think was the most important thing that happened to you, or the most important thing you learned?

followUps: Do you think the person you were then would recognise the person you are now?
Is there anything from that time that you're proud of, or that you're glad you did?
What would you want your great-nieces and great-nephews to know about what life was like then?

guide.briefingNote: Start with one specific place, routine, or object rather than a broad life-summary question. Let the interviewee set the pace and stay with the detail that feels most alive to them. If they correct something or take the story in a different direction, treat that as useful information. Silence is fine. You do not need to fill every pause. If photographs or objects come up, use them as gentle prompts rather than proof points.
openingLines: Thanks for making time, Harold. I'm here to hear about your life—where you grew up, the work you did, the people who mattered. Nothing's too small to mention.
I know you've got a lot of history in this area and in that shed of yours. I'd like to piece together what it was all like, day to day.
This is just you and me talking. There's no right answer to any of this—I'm after what you actually remember and what stuck with you.
sensitivityNotes: If something feels uncertain, ask gently and let them correct or reshape it.
If a topic feels tender, pause and follow their lead rather than pressing on.
If memory is vague on dates or order, stay with the detail they do remember.
If they seem tired or want to stop, that is the right moment to pause or finish.
If photographs or objects appear, use them as gentle prompts rather than testing memory against them.

sections.title: Childhood and place
sections.intro: Let's start with where you grew up and what the place felt like day to day.

questions.text: What's the first thing that comes to mind when you think about where you grew up—what was the street or the area like?
What did you do most days as a kid? Where would you be, and who with?
What was the smell or the sound of that place? The thing you'd recognize straight away if you went back.
Were there places you weren't supposed to go, or places that felt dangerous or exciting?

followUps: Can you picture a specific day? What would happen from morning to bedtime?
Did the seasons change things—winter, summer, what was different?
Who else lived nearby? Any neighbors or other kids you remember clearly?
What did your parents or grandparents say about the area? Did they like living there?

sections.title: Family and relationships
sections.intro: Now let's talk about the people around you and what family life was like.

questions.text: Who was at home with you when you were growing up? Tell me about them.
What was your mum or dad like to be around? What do you remember about how they were with you?
Did you have brothers or sisters? What was that like?
Was there anyone else—a grandparent, aunt, uncle—who was important to you back then?

followUps: What would your dad do in the evenings or at weekends?
How did your mum manage things at home? What was she like when she was busy or worried?
Did you fight with your siblings, or were you close? What do you remember about that?
Was there someone who taught you something, or who you wanted to be like?

sections.title: Work and daily life
sections.intro: I'd like to hear about how you spent your days, and about any work that became part of your life.

questions.text: When did you start working, and what was the first job you did?
How did you get into electrical work? Was that something you always wanted to do, or did it just happen?
What was a typical day like as an electrician? Walk me through it.
Did you enjoy the work? What was good about it, and what was hard?

followUps: Who taught you the trade? What was that like, learning from them?
Were there jobs you remember clearly—maybe something tricky or something that went wrong?
Did you work for yourself or for a company? How did that shape things?
Did the work change over the years? What was different by the time you stopped?

sections.title: Hard times and major events
sections.intro: Every life has stretches that are harder or more uncertain. If any come to mind, we can talk about those gently.

questions.text: Were there times when things felt uncertain or difficult—maybe with work, or at home, or just life?
Did anything happen that changed things for you or your family in a big way?
Looking back, is there anything you wish had gone differently, or anything you had to get through?

followUps: How did you manage when things were tight? What did you do?
Who helped you through it, if anyone did?
Did it change how you saw things, or how you did things after?

sections.title: Objects, photos and remembered stories
sections.intro: Sometimes a photograph, keepsake, or family story unlocks detail that nothing else does.

questions.text: You've kept a lot of things in the shed—tools, photos, bits and pieces. What made you hold onto those things?
Is there a photograph that means something to you? What's in it, and why does it matter?
Do you have a favorite tool, or something you used a lot in your work that you've kept?
Is there a story that gets told in your family—something people mention or that you find yourself telling?

followUps: When did you last look at those photos? What do you think about when you do?
Where did that tool come from? How long have you had it?
Who else was there when that story happened, or who do you tell it to?
Is there something in the shed you'd show me if I asked? What would that be?

sections.title: Looking back
sections.intro: As you look back now, I'd like to hear what stands out and what still feels important.

questions.text: If you think about your whole life so far, what feels like it mattered most?
What are you proud of—things you did or the way you handled something?
What do you want people to know about you, or to remember?

followUps: Is there something you'd do differently if you could go back?
What's been the best part of your life, do you think?
What do you hope for the people coming after you—your grandchildren and beyond?
